danger_signals.visual_form: Track boundaries, wall proximity indicators, other vehicle positions
danger_signals.meaning: Physical danger to vehicle integrity and race position

danger_signals.visual_form: Rain, fog, or adverse weather visualization on track
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental danger increasing crash risk and reducing grip

danger_signals.visual_form: HUD indicators showing tire wear and fuel levels
Other F1 vehicles close to player car, relative position indicators
danger_signals.meaning: Risk of collision and loss of position

safety_signals.visual_form: Open road with visible racing line
safety_signals.meaning: Safe driving conditions, opportunity for acceleration

safety_signals.visual_form: Pit entry markers and pit crew ready state
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone for vehicle maintenance and strategy adjustment

safety_signals.visual_form: HUD readouts showing fresh tires and full fuel tank
safety_signals.meaning: Vehicle is in peak performance condition

safety_signals.visual_form: No competitors visible ahead on track
safety_signals.meaning: Control of race and reduced collision risk

faction_indicators.color_identity: Team-specific livery colors (McLaren red/silver, Ferrari rosso corsa, Williams FW20 white/blue, etc.)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Team badges and sponsor logos on vehicle bodywork
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Competing teams' distinctive paint schemes and aerodynamic designs
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Pit crew uniforms matching team colors, team garage appearance

faction_indicators.color_identity: Engine manufacturer identity (Ferrari, Mercedes, Renault, Honda visual cues)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Rival constructor vehicles with different aerodynamic profiles
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Technical specifications reflected in vehicle handling and performance characteristics

faction_indicators.color_identity: National color conventions (British Racing Green, Italian red, German silver historically referenced)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Driver nationality flags in championship standings and HUD
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Different national driver lineups in multiplayer and career modes
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Championship standings displaying driver home nations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Track conditions pristine, minimal rubber on track surface, clear visibility
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: AI driver behavior more experimental, testing setup variations, higher error rates

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Track surface shows rubber buildup, dust and debris patterns, tire marks accumulate
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: AI drivers settle into race rhythm, competitive positioning stabilizes, strategic pit stops occur

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Track heavily marked with tire wear patterns, debris concentrated in problem areas, lighting may shift with time-of-day simulation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: AI aggression increases, desperation passing attempts, fuel/tire management becomes critical

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Points standings display prominently, driver and constructor championship tables updated visually
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: AI drivers adjust risk tolerance based on championship position, leading drivers play conservatively, trailing drivers take more risks

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Rain effects on screen, visibility reduction, track surface changes from dry to wet with darker asphalt appearance
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: AI drivers slow down, take wider racing lines, pit for intermediate or wet tires, increased caution in turns

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Visual damage model on player vehicle (wing damage, paint damage, visible mechanical failure)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Handling characteristics degrade, acceleration and braking affected, AI drivers may be forced into pit stops for repairs

environmental_storytelling.example: Monaco's narrow streets with tight barriers tell story of precision required; Monza's long straights indicate power-dependent circuits; Suzuka's technical layout emphasizes driver skill
Team pit boxes decorated with team colors and sponsor branding; pit crew positioned and costumed according to real F1 protocols; refueling and tire-change procedures visualized
2000 Season expansion updates vehicle aerodynamics and liveries, showing technical evolution; new circuits (Sepang, Indianapolis) added to calendar reflecting real-world F1 expansion
Qualifying position board and race grid arrangement show competitive hierarchy; championship points accumulation visible through season progress
Car setup menus and telemetry displays show downforce levels, brake balance, and tire compounds—technical realism communicates simulation depth

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Conservative racing line, defensive positioning on straights, blocks overtaking attempts, manages tire wear
Aggressive racing, takes calculated risks, attempts overtakes on straights, responds to competitor moves

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Out of championship contention or new season start
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: High-risk passing attempts, erratic line choices, occasional contact, rapid pit strategy changes
Coordinated tire changes and refueling animation, crew members appear in proper positioning, speed of pit stop affects race outcome
Emerges on track, bunches field together, controls pace, leads parade lap until incident cleared
Visual deformation, handling degradation, smoke or fluid leaks, reduced performance characteristics

notes: Grand Prix 3 is a high-fidelity racing simulation (T2 tier) based on 1998 F1 season. Wikipedia source provides limited information on visual manifestation systems; data extracted emphasizes racing simulation paradigm conventions. The game's communication layer relies heavily on F1 authentic visual identity (team liveries, real driver names, licensed circuits, sponsor branding) to convey world-state. Manifestation of world-state occurs primarily through: (1) vehicle visual differentiation by team and driver, (2) track condition changes (rubber buildup, weather), (3) HUD telemetry displays, (4) championship standings progression, (5) damage modeling on vehicles. Danger/safety signals are driven by racing mechanics (proximity, resources, track hazards) rather than narrative faction conflict. The paradigm shift from typical game genres means manifestation patterns focus on competitive hierarchy and technical systems rather than environmental storytelling or player threat assessment.
